title: Relevance — Routine Referral to Gastroenterologist
brief: You are a GP referring Mrs Helen Carr (62) to a gastroenterologist for assessment and management of suspected IBS with possible bile-acid diarrhoea. Decide which case notes the specialist actually needs.

scenario.purpose: Further investigation and definitive diagnosis of suspected IBS / bile-acid diarrhoea

notes.text: 6-month history of intermittent loose stools, post-prandial cramping, urgency.
notes.rationale: Core presenting complaint — defines the referral question.

notes.text: No PR bleeding, no nocturnal diarrhoea, no weight loss.
notes.rationale: Negative red-flag screen — gastroenterologist needs this to triage.
Surgical history directly relevant to suspected bile-acid diarrhoea.
Loperamide is symptom-modifying; metformin can cause diarrhoea — both must be stated.
Allergy is required for atopic conditions; here it is borderline — either choice is defensible.

notes.text: Smokes 5 cigarettes/day; drinks 2 units wine on weekends.
notes.rationale: Smoking and drinking are always included for medical recipients (Rule R03.4).

notes.text: Owns a Persian cat called Whiskers.
notes.rationale: Pet name is irrelevant to a gastroenterology referral.
Family/living situation irrelevant unless directly affecting management.
No GI family history — not relevant to this referral.

notes.text: Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses, normal bowel sounds.
notes.rationale: Reassuring abdominal exam — relevant to triage.

notes.text: FBC, U&E, LFTs, TFTs, coeliac screen, FIT — all normal.
notes.rationale: Already-completed work-up the specialist must not duplicate.

notes.text: Random urinalysis 2014 — trace protein, repeat normal.
Refer for further investigation; consider SeHCAT scan and colonoscopy.
notes.rationale: States the referral request — must be in the closing paragraph.
